1: "The word of the LORD came to me" — the standard prophetic reception formula opening the covenant-lawsuit section (Jeremiah 2-3). This formula recurs throughout Jeremiah as the structural signal of a new divine address. Christologically, every instance of the word of YHWH coming to Jeremiah is a subordinate form of the final, definitive coming of the Word in Christ. As Hebrews 1:1-2 frames it: "Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets" — each formula-instance is one of those many-times — "but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." The repetition of the reception formula across Jeremiah's forty years underscores the accumulation of partial speech that points to the final and complete speaking in Christ.
2: "I remember the devotion of your youth, the love of your bridal days — how you followed me through the wilderness." YHWH's covenant memory (zāḵartî) of the betrothal period — Israel's following YHWH through the wilderness — establishes the marriage metaphor that governs Jeremiah 2-3. Christologically, this divine remembering of covenant love finds its eschatological fulfillment in Christ as the Bridegroom who loved his bride before she existed. Ephesians 5:25-27 applies this pre-temporal love to Christ: "Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her." The bridal devotion that Israel failed to sustain, the church sustains through Christ's own faithfulness imputed to her. Revelation 19:7-8 ("the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his Bride has made herself ready") is the restoration of exactly this bridal devotion, achieved through the Bridegroom's own work.
3: "Israel was holy to the LORD, the firstfruits of his harvest." The qōdeš (holy, set apart) status of Israel as YHWH's rēʾšît (firstfruits) positions Israel as the dedicated portion belonging exclusively to YHWH. Those who consume what is dedicated to YHWH incur guilt. Christologically, Christ is himself the ʾappîryon rēʾšît (firstfruits) of the new creation — the resurrected firstfruit of those who have died (1 Corinthians 15:20, 23). Israel's firstfruits status was provisional and violated; Christ's firstfruits status is permanent and never violated. The church, in Christ, participates in his firstfruits consecration (James 1:18: "a kind of firstfruits of his creatures").
4: "Hear the word of the LORD, O house of Jacob, all the families of Israel" — the formal summons to the covenant lawsuit (rîḇ) opening. The legal address to "house of Jacob... all the families of Israel" encompasses the entire covenant people. Christologically, the same summons to hear the divine word reaches its most urgent form in the words of Jesus: "Truly, truly I say to you..." (the Johannine amen amen) and in the Synoptic "He who has ears, let him hear" (Matthew 11:15; Jeremiah 13:9, 43). The prophetic call to hear the LORD's word is intensified in Christ because the speaker is himself the Word: to hear Christ is to hear the Father directly (John 14:9).
5: "What fault did your fathers find in me, that they went so far from me, following worthless idols and becoming worthless themselves?" The rhetorical question of the divine plaintiff invites Israel to state her charges against YHWH — the implication being that none can be produced. YHWH is faultless; Israel's abandonment is groundless. Christologically, this same groundless abandonment reaches its climactic expression in John's passion narrative: the crowds choose Barabbas over Jesus (John 18:38-40), and Pilate — echoing the divine plaintiff — can find no fault in him (John 18:38: "I find no guilt in him at all"). The Jeremianic covenant lawsuit is the OT form of the Johannine passion trial: the faultless one is rejected without cause.
6: "They did not ask: Where is the LORD who brought us up out of Egypt, who led us through the wilderness?" The failure to ask after YHWH — to seek him, to inquire of his presence — is the covenant violation that underlies all the others. The wilderness guidance (pillar of cloud/fire, the manna, the water from the rock) is the specific divine provision forgotten. Christologically, Paul identifies the wilderness-provision with Christ explicitly: "all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ" (1 Corinthians 10:4). Israel's failure to ask "Where is YHWH who provided?" is therefore a failure to seek Christ himself, who was present in the wilderness provision. The seeking that Israel failed to do is the seeking Jesus invites: "Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find" (Matthew 7:7).
7: "I brought you into a land of plenty, to enjoy its fruit and abundance. But when you entered, you defiled my land and made my heritage loathsome." The gift of the land — YHWH's inheritance given to Israel — is defiled by the very recipients. Christologically, this pattern of gift-defiled-by-recipients is the universal human story that only Christ breaks. Adam received the garden and defiled it; Israel received the land and defiled it; but Christ receives the inheritance of all creation (Psalms 2:8; Hebrews 1:2: "appointed the heir of all things") and does not defile it. The defilement-of-inheritance pattern anticipates the need for a new Adam and a new Israel who will receive the inheritance faithfully — which is Christ's role (Romans 5:17-19; 1 Corinthians 15:45-49).
8: "The priests did not ask, 'Where is the LORD?'... the prophets prophesied by Baal." Every appointed guardian of the covenant has failed: priests, Torah-teachers, rulers, and prophets alike. The comprehensive leadership failure creates a vacuum of faithful covenant mediation. Christologically, this vacuum is precisely what Christ fills as the new and better covenant mediator: he is simultaneously the faithful High Priest who always asks after the Father (John 11:41-42; Jeremiah 17:1-26), the true Prophet who speaks only what the Father has given him (John 12:49-50), and the righteous King who executes justice (Revelation 19:11). Where every human covenant official failed, Christ alone fulfills all three offices — the threefold office (prophet, priest, king) is the Christological answer to the threefold institutional failure of v8.
9: "Therefore I will bring charges against you again... and against your grandchildren will I bring charges." The intergenerational scope of the covenant lawsuit — extending through generations — is the legal form of what Paul addresses in Rom 1-3: the universal human condition of covenant violation, cumulative and intergenerational. The need for an answer to this intergenerational indictment is the need that Christ's work addresses: his obedience is credited to all generations of faith (Romans 5:18-19; Galatians 3:13-14). The legal charges are answered not by human defense but by the one who takes the covenant-violation penalty himself.
10: "Cross to the shores of Cyprus and look; send to Kedar and examine carefully." The invitation to a cross-cultural survey — from Cyprus (westernmost of the ANE) to Kedar (Arab tribes to the east) — establishes the universality of the charge: no nation has abandoned its gods. Christologically, this universal survey anticipates the NT's universal proclamation. The nations that were examined for loyalty to their false gods are the same nations to whom Christ is proclaimed as the living God (Acts 17:22-31). What the pagan nations did not do for their idols — abandon them — the church does for Christ: holds to him despite all opposition (Philippians 1:27-28).
11: "Has any nation changed its gods, even though they are not real gods? But my people have exchanged their glory for things that are worthless." The irony is cutting: pagan nations at least maintain loyalty to their non-existent gods, while Israel abandons the real and living God for nonentities. kĕḇôdām (their glory) — Israel has exchanged YHWH himself, who is their glory (Psalms 106:20 quotes this verse directly). Christologically, Paul applies exactly this exchange to the universal human condition in Romans 1:23 ("exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images"), reading Jeremiah 2:11 as the diagnosis of the universal human problem that only the revelation of Christ's glory answers (2 Corinthians 4:6: "the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ").
12: "Be appalled at this, O heavens; shudder with great horror." The call for the heavens to witness and react to Israel's apostasy invokes the cosmic lawsuit of Deuteronomy 32:1 ("Give ear, O heavens, and I will speak"). The heavens as witnesses in the covenant lawsuit are a standard OT legal convention. Christologically, this cosmic horror at covenant violation finds its ultimate expression at the cross, where the heavens respond to the crucifixion: the midday darkness (Matthew 27:45), the earthquake, and the torn veil (Matthew 27:51-52). The cosmic šāmmâ (horror) provoked by Israel's covenant violation in Jeremiah is fulfilled and surpassed by the cosmic response to the one who bore that violation in himself.
13: "My people have committed two evils: they have abandoned me, the spring of living water, and dug their own cisterns — cracked cisterns that cannot hold water." The most Christologically explicit verse in Jeremiah 2. Jesus applies both sides of the contrast to himself in conversation with the Samaritan woman: he is the one who gives "living water" (mayim ḥayyîm) that becomes "a spring of water welling up to eternal life" (John 4:10-14). In John 7:37-38, Jesus stands at the Feast of Tabernacles — the water-drawing festival — and cries: "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink... out of his heart will flow rivers of living water." The spring of living water that Israel abandoned in Jeremiah 2:13 is identified as Christ himself. The cracked cisterns of human religious self-sufficiency (whether idolatry or second-temple Judaism) are contrasted with the inexhaustible spring that is Christ. Revelation 22:1 ("the river of the water of life... flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb") is the eschatological fulfillment of Jeremiah 2:13's positive image.
14: "Is Israel a slave? Was he born into slavery? Then why has he become plunder?" The rhetorical questions address the scandal of Israel's historical vulnerability — given her covenant status as YHWH's firstborn (Exodus 4:22), why is she treated like a slave? Christologically, this paradox of the covenant son becoming a slave anticipates Philippians 2:6-8 (Christ "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but... taking the form of a servant"). The Son who should be free from slavery becomes a slave — but in Christ's case, voluntarily, for redemptive purpose. Galatians 4:4-5 makes the connection explicit: God sent his Son "born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law, so that we might receive adoption as sons."
15: "Young lions have roared against him and growled; they have laid his land waste; his cities have been burned." The image of marauding lions destroying the land is the military-desolation metaphor. Christologically, the lion imagery has a double resonance in the NT: Peter warns that the devil "prowls around like a roaring lion" (1 Peter 5:8), using the same image for destructive opposition; but Revelation 5:5 presents Christ as "the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David" who has conquered — reversing the imagery. The lion that destroys Israel becomes, in Christ, the Lion who defeats all enemies.
16: "The people of Memphis and Tahpanhes have shaved the top of your head." Egypt's treatment of Judah — the head-shaving as sign of disgrace and subjugation — is the immediate geopolitical humiliation. Christologically, the head-shaving (crown-of-head dishonor) anticipates the crown of thorns placed on Christ at his passion (Matthew 27:29; John 19:2-3). The dishonor inflicted on the covenant people foreshadows the dishonor inflicted on the covenant representative — except that in Christ's case, the dishonor is reversed into glory (Philippians 2:9-11).
17: "Have you not brought this on yourself by forsaking the LORD your God when he led you on the way?" The rhetorical question places the responsibility for the covenant disaster squarely on Israel's own unfaithfulness — the self-caused suffering of forsaking the divine guide. Christologically, Christ embodies the reversal: rather than Israel who forsook the divine guide on "the way," Christ declares himself to be "the way" (John 14:6) and never departs from it. The disciples who forsook Jesus at Gethsemane (Matthew 26:56) re-enact the Jeremianic pattern; the resurrection reverses it, as Christ pursues and restores the scattered disciples (John 21:1-19).
18: "Why go down to Egypt to drink from the waters of the Nile? Or why go to Assyria to drink from the Euphrates?" The double question addresses Judah's political strategy of seeking alliances with the two great powers rather than trusting YHWH. Drinking from the Nile or Euphrates is political dependence dressed as wisdom. Christologically, this false seeking of foreign water is the foil to Jesus's offer at the well: "Everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again" (John 4:13-14). The choice between the Nile/Euphrates and the spring of living water is the OT form of the choice between all human resources and Christ.
19: "Your own wickedness will punish you, and your turning away will rebuke you." The principle of intrinsic moral consequence — sin as its own punishment — is a consistent wisdom theme (Proverbs 1:31; Jeremiah 5:22). Christologically, Galatians 6:7-8 ("whatever a man sows, that he will also reap") formulates the same principle. But the gospel's radical move is that Christ enters into the consequence of others' sowing — he who sowed only righteousness reaps the harvest of others' wickedness (Galatians 3:13: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us"). The intrinsic-consequence principle of Jeremiah 2:19 is not negated by the cross but absorbed into it: Christ's death is the enacted consequence of humanity's turning away.
20: "For long ago I broke your yoke and tore off your chains. But you said, 'I will not serve!'" The exodus liberation — YHWH breaking Israel's yoke of slavery in Egypt — is met with refusal to serve YHWH as liberator. The paradox: freed from one yoke, Israel refuses the yoke that is the covenant. Christologically, Jesus offers a different yoke: "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you... for my yoke is easy and my burden is light" (Matthew 11:28-30). The refusal to serve YHWH in Jeremiah 2:20 is the form of the same rebellion against Christ's gentle yoke. The difference: the new covenant yoke is borne by the one who offers it — Christ carries his own yoke.
21: "I had planted you as a choice vine (śōrēq), of pure and wholesome seed. How then have you turned against me into the wild shoots of a foreign vine?" The vineyard imagery is one of the most developed Christological types in the OT. Isaiah 5:1-7 expands it into the Song of the Vineyard (the beloved's vineyard producing wild grapes). Jesus explicitly takes up this imagery in John 15:1: "I am the true vine" (hē ampelos hē alēthine), deliberately contrasting himself with the false vine of Israel's covenant failure. The choice vine planted by YHWH in Jeremiah 2:21 but producing wild growth is answered by Christ who is himself the true vine producing true fruit. John 15:5 ("I am the vine; you are the branches") completes the metaphor: the restoration of Israel-as-vine happens through union with Christ-as-vine.
22: "Even if you scrubbed yourself with lye and used much soap, the stain of your guilt would still be before me." The indelibility of moral guilt — no human cleansing agent removes the māʾôr ʿăwōnēḵ (stain of your iniquity) — is the diagnosis that only the cross answers. Christologically, Hebrews 9:13-14 contrasts the ritual purification of the Levitical system with the blood of Christ: "How much more will the blood of Christ... purify our conscience from dead works." 1 John 1:7 ("the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin") is the direct answer to Jeremiah 2:22's impossible standard. The cleansing that lye and soap cannot achieve, Christ's atoning death accomplishes.
23: "How can you say, 'I am not defiled, I have not chased after the Baals'?" The self-justification that denies evident guilt — the animal imagery of the camel and wild donkey (vv23-24) developing the point — is the moral blindness that accompanies persistent sin. Christologically, this self-justification is what Jesus confronts in the Pharisees (Luke 18:9-14: the self-justifying Pharisee and the tax collector who knows his guilt) and in the rich young ruler (Matthew 19:16-22: the one who claims to have kept all the commandments). The self-deception of Jeremiah 2:23 is not merely Israel's problem but the universal human condition that the NT identifies as the prerequisite blindness to grace.
24: "Like a wild donkey at home in the desert, sniffing the wind in her craving — who can hold her back when she is in heat?" The wild donkey image for uncontrollable sexual desire applied to Israel's idolatry is a deliberately shocking metaphor for covenant unfaithfulness. The inability to hold back (yiśśĕrennāh, Qal imperfect, who can restrain her?) describes the compulsive quality of idolatrous desire. Christologically, Romans 1:24-27 describes exactly this compulsive, craving disorder as the consequence of idolatry: God "gave them up to dishonorable passions" as the judicial consequence of exchanging the Creator for the creature. The wild-donkey craving of Jeremiah 2:24 is the OT image of what Paul diagnoses as the theological root of human sexual disorder.
25: "But you said: It is hopeless! I love other gods, and after them I will go." The declaration of hopeless addiction to false gods — "it is hopeless (nôʾāš)" — is the voice of entrenched covenant rebellion. The despair of the one who has abandoned YHWH and cannot conceive of return is the condition Paul describes in Ephesians 2:12 ("without hope and without God in the world"). Christologically, the gospel's answer to this despairing self-assessment is the hope offered in Christ: "You who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ" (Ephesians 2:13). The hopelessness of Jeremiah 2:25 is answered not by human self-reform but by the divine initiative in the cross.
26: "As a thief is put to shame when caught, so the house of Israel will be shamed — they, their kings, their officials, their priests, and their prophets." The comprehensive shame covering all Israel's leadership is the diagnosis that makes the new covenant's comprehensive address necessary. Christologically, the shame (bōšet) that covers the entire community — every official level, every institution — is what Christ absorbs at the cross (Hebrews 12:2: "who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame"). The communal shame of Jeremiah 2:26 is answered by Christ's personal assumption of shame, and his resurrection is the reversal of that shame into glory.
27: "They say to a tree, 'You are my father,' and to a stone, 'You gave me birth.' They have turned their backs to me and not their faces. Yet in the time of their trouble they will cry, 'Rise up and save us!'" The hypocrisy is complete: YHWH is addressed only in crisis, while trees and stones receive the paternal/natal devotion that belongs to him. The crisis-prayer to YHWH after devoted loyalty to idols is the pattern Jesus addresses in Matthew 7:21-23 ("Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom"). The turning of backs in normal time and faces only in trouble is the OT form of the false discipleship Christ identifies as dangerous. The authentic relationship involves facing toward God not only in crisis but continuously — which only the indwelling Spirit of the new covenant enables (John 14:17).
28: "But where are the gods you made for yourself? Let them rise up, if they can rescue you in the time of your trouble." The challenge to idols to rise up (yāqûmû) and rescue is the theological polemic that frames the entire OAN tradition. Christologically, this challenge is answered by Christ's resurrection: the one YHWH raised from the dead does in fact save in the time of trouble. Acts 2:24 ("God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death") and Romans 10:9 ("if you... believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved") present the resurrection as the definitive answer to every "can your god rise up and save?" challenge in the OT. The idols cannot rise; Christ does.
29: "Why do you bring a complaint against me? You have all rebelled against me." YHWH as the defendant in Israel's counter-suit — she accuses YHWH of injustice even as she has rebelled — is the rhetorical posture of covenant inversion. Paul addresses the same counter-accusation in Romans 9:19-21 ("Why does he still find fault? Who can resist his will?") and in the diatribe of Rom 2-3. The answer in both Jeremiah and Paul is the same: the charge of divine injustice is groundless because the covenant violation was entirely human. Romans 3:4 ("Let God be true though every man be a liar") is the Pauline form of Jeremiah 2:29.
30: "I have struck your people in vain; they accepted no correction. Your own sword has consumed your prophets like a ravaging lion." The killing of YHWH's prophets by Israel's own sword is the culminating indictment of v30 and is the pattern Jesus identifies as the characteristic sin of Jerusalem. Matthew 23:37: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those who are sent to you!" — a direct citation of the Jeremianic pattern. Acts 7:52 (Stephen's speech) makes the same charge: "Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute?" The sword that consumed the prophets in Jeremiah 2:30 is the same sword that will be raised against Christ, the last and greatest of the sent prophets (and more than a prophet).
31: "O generation, take heed of the word of the LORD: Have I been a desert to Israel or a land of thick darkness? Why do my people say, 'We are free; we will come to you no more'?" The divine challenge — YHWH as the one who has provided, not withheld — uses the wilderness metaphor: has YHWH been midbār (desert, wilderness, deprivation) to Israel? The answer is no; YHWH has been the spring of living water (v13). Christologically, Jesus's claim "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst" (John 6:35) is the self-application of YHWH's challenge in Jeremiah 2:31. The declaration "We will come to you no more" is the ultimate form of covenant rejection that finds its NT form in those who "went away" from Jesus (John 6:66).
32: "Does a young woman forget her jewelry, or a bride her wedding attire? Yet my people have forgotten me — for more days than can be counted." The absurdity of Israel's forgetfulness — more remarkable than a bride forgetting her wedding dress — is the rhetorical vehicle for the depth of the betrayal. Christologically, this contrast between bridal devotion and Israel's forgetfulness anticipates the church's calling to undivided devotion to Christ as the bride to her husband. Paul uses the bridal imagery in 2 Corinthians 11:2: "I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ." The church's forgetting of Christ would be the NT counterpart of Israel's forgetting — which is why Paul's pastoral letters constantly recall, remind, and re-present the gospel.
33: "How skilled you are at pursuing love; you have become expert enough to teach the wicked your ways." The ironic praise of Israel's expertise in pursuing false loves — she has become a teacher of wickedness — inverts the covenant calling to be a light to the nations (Isaiah 49:6). Instead of teaching the nations YHWH's ways, Israel has become a curriculum in unfaithfulness. Christologically, Christ reverses this irony: where Israel taught wickedness, Christ is himself the way, truth, and life that teaches the nations (Matthew 28:20: "teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you"). The church's vocation to teach the nations is grounded in Christ's own teaching authority, not in human expertise.
34: "On your garments is found the lifeblood of innocent poor people — you did not catch them breaking in." The blood of innocent people — wrongfully shed outside of the self-defense exception of Exodus 22:2-3 — is on Israel's hands. The shedding of innocent blood defiles the land (Numbers 35:33-34). Christologically, Matthew 27:24-25 presents Pilate washing his hands of "innocent blood" (the only NT use of haima athōon, lit. "innocent blood") while the crowd accepts responsibility. Jesus is the innocent one whose blood is shed — but unlike the innocent poor of Jeremiah 2:34, whose blood condemns those who shed it, Christ's innocent blood is the means of covenant cleansing (Hebrews 9:14; 1 John 1:7).
35: "Yet you say, 'I am innocent; surely his anger has turned from me.' But I will bring judgment against you because you say, 'I have not sinned.'" The refusal to acknowledge sin — the double self-justification ("I am innocent" and "I have not sinned") — is the state that makes repentance impossible. 1 John 1:8-10 directly addresses this: "If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves... If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins." The covenant lawsuit's requirement that the defendant acknowledge guilt is the OT form of the NT's requirement of confession for forgiveness. Christ's work makes forgiveness possible; the acknowledgment of sin makes it accessible.
36: "Why do you rush about so much, changing course? You will be disappointed by Egypt just as you were disappointed by Assyria." The political strategy of cycling through great-power alliances — Egypt to Assyria and back — is the foreign-policy form of spiritual unfaithfulness. Neither alliance saves; neither power proves reliable. Christologically, this disappointment by all human powers and alliances is the universal experience that drives the seeking of the divine king. Psalms 146:3-5 ("Put not your trust in princes... blessed is he whose help is the God of Jacob") makes the theological application; Jesus's kingship is the one power that never disappoints (Romans 10:11: "everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame").
37: "You will leave Egypt too with your hands on your head in defeat, for the LORD has rejected the ones you are trusting." The hands-on-head gesture (yādêḵā ʿal-rōʾšēḵ) is the posture of total humiliation and defeat (2 Samuel 13:19; Tamar's grief). YHWH rejects the objects of Israel's trust — the great empires she courted — because he is the only trustworthy power. Christologically, this rejection of false trust-objects is the theological ground of Christ's exclusive claim. John 14:6 ("No one comes to the Father except through me") is not merely soteriological exclusivism but a statement about the trustworthiness of all other refuges: hands on heads, all of them fail. Only Christ, rejected and crucified, is vindicated by the Father and established as the sole reliable foundation (1 Peter 2:6).
